does: This paper conducts a systematic review and classification of jailbreak attacks and defenses against large language models, developing Security Cube, a multi-dimensional evaluation framework that moves beyond single-metric assessment. The authors establish a comprehensive taxonomy of attack methods and defense strategies, then benchmark 13 representative jailbreak attacks against 5 defense mechanisms. Rather than proposing new attacks or defenses, the work synthesizes the fragmented literature by providing detailed comparison tables, identifying key patterns in how adversarial prompts exploit model vulnerabilities, and demonstrating that traditional metrics like attack success rate inadequately capture the complex security landscape of LLMs.
connects: This work builds on growing research into adversarial robustness in machine learning, extending prior work on adversarial examples in vision to the language domain where jailbreaking has emerged as a critical safety problem. It responds to limitations in earlier evaluation methodologies from adversarial ML that relied on narrow metrics, drawing lessons from multidimensional security frameworks used in system security and cryptography. The paper engages with concurrent work on prompt injection attacks, adversarial prompting techniques, and various defense mechanisms including input filtering, output monitoring, and fine-tuning approaches that have accumulated across recent LLM safety literature.
enables: This systematic organization and evaluation framework establishes a foundation for developing more robust defenses by clarifying which attacks remain unaddressed and where defense gaps exist. Security Cube enables future researchers to design and evaluate defenses against multiple attack vectors simultaneously rather than in isolation, supporting the development of adversarially-resistant LLM training methods. The comprehensive benchmark creates a standard evaluation methodology that could facilitate reproducible research and aid regulatory compliance efforts. This work points toward developing defense strategies that handle novel attack variants, understanding the theoretical limits of jailbreak resistance, and integrating security considerations into model pretraining rather than relying solely on post-hoc mitigations.
